name: radial basis function
best_quote: Each of the hm is a radial basis function h(x) = D(||x - μm||), where D is some distance function (usually Euclidean).

name: kernel smoother
best_quote: These methods specify the nature of the local neighborhood explicitly by using a kernel weighting function K_λ(x_0, x), which assigns weights to points x in a region around x_0.

name: supervised learning
best_quote: For each there is a set of variables that might be denoted as inputs, which are measured or preset. These have some influence on one or more outputs. For each example the goal is to use the inputs to predict the values of the outputs. This exercise is called supervised learning.

name: training data
best_quote: We thus suppose we have available a set of measurements (x_i, y_i) or (x_i, g_i), i = 1, . . . , N, known as the training data, with which to construct our prediction rule.

name: bias in machine learning
best_quote: The term β̂_0 is the intercept, also known as the bias in machine learning.

name: regression function
best_quote: the conditional expectation, also known as the regression function. Thus the best prediction of Y at any point X = x is the conditional mean, when best is measured by average squared error.

name: Bayes rate
best_quote: The error rate of the Bayes classifier is called the Bayes rate.

name: Bayes classifier
best_quote: This reasonable solution is known as the Bayes classifier, and says that we classify to the most probable class, using the conditional (discrete) distribution Pr(G|X).

name: activation function
best_quote: where σ(x) = 1/(1 + e^{-x}) is known as the activation function.

name: loss function
best_quote: To proceed, we need a loss function L(Y, f(X)) for penalizing errors in prediction

name: squared error loss
best_quote: By far the most common and convenient is squared error loss: L(Y, f(X)) = (Y − f(X))^2

name: absolute error loss
best_quote: Absolute error loss L(Y, f(X)) = |Y − f(X)|

name: conditional expectation
best_quote: the conditional expectation, also known as the regression function

name: conditional median
best_quote: The conditional median is the optimal predictor under absolute error loss

name: bayes decision boundary
best_quote: Figure 2.5 shows the Bayes-optimal decision boundary

name: least squares fitting
best_quote: we fit this model by least squares

name: residual sum of squares
best_quote: Residual sum of squares

name: fitted values
best_quote: The fitted values at the training inputs are

name: k-nearest neighbor averaging
best_quote: The k-nearest neighbor fit for Ŷ is defined as averaging over the k nearest neighbors

name: k-nearest neighbor classifier
best_quote: k-nearest neighbor classifier

name: nearest neighbor decision boundary
best_quote: The decision boundary is irregular

name: curse of dimensionality
best_quote: in high dimensions all feasible training samples sparsely populate the input space

name: sampling density
best_quote: To capture 1% or 10% of the data to form a local average requires a much larger neighborhood in high dimensions

name: empty space phenomenon
best_quote: most data points are closer to a boundary than to any other data point

name: structured regression model
best_quote: We must make some assumptions about the structure of the regression function

name: linear model assumption
best_quote: The linear model makes huge assumptions about structure

name: additive model
best_quote: An additive model assumes the regression function has the form

name: linear combination model
best_quote: linear combination model

name: interaction term
best_quote: We can include interaction terms and other derived features

name: penalty functional
best_quote: The user-selected functional J(f) will be large for functions f that vary too rapidly

name: smoothing parameter
best_quote: The smoothing parameter λ establishes the tradeoff between the residual sum of squares and the penalty

name: kernel width
best_quote: The width of the kernel function is a smoothing parameter

name: nadaraya-watson estimator
best_quote: The Nadaraya-Watson weighted average

name: locally weighted regression
best_quote: locally weighted least squares

name: basis function expansion
best_quote: represent f as a linear expansion of basis functions

name: sigmoid basis function
best_quote: sigmoid basis functions

name: dictionary
best_quote: dictionary D of candidate basis functions

name: model complexity
best_quote: As we increase the model complexity, the variance tends to increase and the squared bias tends to decrease

name: degrees of freedom
best_quote: degrees of freedom

name: irreducible error
best_quote: irreducible error

name: optimism of training error
best_quote: Training error tends to decrease as model complexity increases, but test error does not

name: out-of-sample error
best_quote: out-of-sample error

name: expected test error
best_quote: the expected test error (also known as EPE)

name: pointwise prediction error
best_quote: error at a specific point x_0

name: misclassification error
best_quote: the probability of misclassification

name: posterior probability
best_quote: Pr(G=k|X=x) is the conditional probability of class k

name: nearest neighbor region
best_quote: the nearest neighbor region

name: k-nearest neighbor boundary
best_quote: The decision boundary is irregular with k=1 but becomes smoother as k increases

name: dimension reduction
best_quote: dimension reduction techniques

name: projection method
best_quote: projection methods

name: least squares projection
best_quote: least squares is a projection method

name: model selection criterion
best_quote: model selection criterion

name: training set validation
best_quote: validation

name: cross-validation error
best_quote: cross-validation

name: prior distribution
best_quote: prior distribution

name: bayesian framework
best_quote: Bayesian methods

name: squared bias
best_quote: the squared bias tends to decrease

name: variance of prediction
best_quote: As we increase the model complexity, the variance tends to increase

name: Lasso
best_quote: Because of the nature of the constraint, making t sufficiently small will cause some of the coefficients to be exactly zero. Thus the lasso does a kind of continuous subset selection.

name: gauss-markov theorem
best_quote: The Gauss–Markov theorem states that if we have any other linear estimator θ̃ = c^T y that is unbiased for a^T β, that is, E(c^T y) = a^T β, then Var(a^T β̂) ≤ Var(c^T y).

name: orthogonal projection
best_quote: We minimize RSS(β) = ||y − Xβ||^2 by choosing β̂ so that the residual vector y − ŷ is orthogonal to this subspace. This orthogonality is expressed in (3.5), and the resulting estimate ŷ is hence the orthogonal projection of y onto this subspace.

name: qr decomposition
best_quote: the so-called QR decomposition of X. Here Q is an N × (p + 1) orthogonal matrix, Q^T Q = I, and R is a (p + 1) × (p + 1) upper triangular matrix.

name: gram-schmidt orthogonalization
best_quote: Algorithm 3.1 is known as the Gram–Schmidt procedure for multiple regression, and is also a useful numerical strategy for computing the estimates.

name: best subset selection
best_quote: Best subset regression finds for each k ∈{0, 1, 2, . . . , p} the subset of size k that gives smallest residual sum of squares (3.2).

name: leaps and bounds procedure
best_quote: An efficient algorithm—the leaps and bounds procedure (Furnival and Wilson, 1974)—makes this feasible for p as large as 30 or 40.

name: forward stepwise selection
best_quote: Forward-stepwise selection starts with the intercept, and then sequentially adds into the model the predictor that most improves the fit.

name: backward stepwise selection
best_quote: Backward-stepwise selection starts with the full model, and sequentially deletes the predictor that has the least impact on the fit. The candidate for dropping is the variable with the smallest Z-score

name: forward stagewise regression
best_quote: At each step the algorithm identifies the variable most correlated with the current residual. It then computes the simple linear regression coefficient of the residual on this chosen variable, and then adds it to the current coefficient for that variable.

name: ridge regression
best_quote: Ridge regression shrinks the regression coefficients by imposing a penalty on their size. The ridge coefficients minimize a penalized residual sum of squares

name: basis pursuit
best_quote: In the signal processing literature, the lasso is also known as basis pursuit (Chen et al., 1998).

name: soft thresholding
best_quote: Lasso translates each coefficient by a constant factor λ, truncating at zero. This is called "soft thresholding," and is used in the context of wavelet-based smoothing

name: least angle regression
best_quote: Least angle regression uses a similar strategy, but only enters "as much" of a predictor as it deserves. At the first step it identifies the variable most correlated with the response. Rather than fit this variable completely, LAR moves the coefficient of this variable continuously toward its least-squares value

name: lar-lasso connection
best_quote: These observations lead to a simple modification of the LAR algorithm that gives the entire lasso path, which is also piecewise-linear.

name: homotopy algorithm
best_quote: Osborne et al. (2000a) also discovered a piecewise-linear path for computing the lasso, which they called a homotopy algorithm.

name: degrees of freedom for adaptive fit
best_quote: We define the degrees of freedom of the fitted vector ŷ = (ŷ_1, ŷ_2, . . . , ŷ_N) as df(ŷ) = sum Cov(ŷ_i, y_i)/σ^2. This makes intuitive sense: the harder that we fit to the data, the larger this covariance and hence df(ŷ).

name: principal components regression
best_quote: Principal component regression forms the derived input columns z_m = Xv_m, and then regresses y on z_1, z_2, . . . , z_M for some M ≤ p.

name: partial least squares
best_quote: Like principal component regression, partial least squares (PLS) is not scale invariant, so we assume that each x_j is standardized to have mean 0 and variance 1. PLS begins by computing φ̂_1j = ⟨x_j, y⟩for each j.

name: canonical correlation analysis
best_quote: Similar to PCA, CCA finds a sequence of uncorrelated linear combinations Xv_m, m = 1, . . . , M of the x_j, and a corresponding sequence of uncorrelated linear combinations Yu_m of the responses y_k, such that the correlations are successively maximized.

name: reduced-rank regression
best_quote: Reduced-rank regression (Izenman, 1975; van der Merwe and Zidek, 1980) formalizes this approach in terms of a regression model that explicitly pools information.

name: curds and whey
best_quote: Breiman and Friedman (1997) explored with some success shrinkage of the canonical variates between X and Y, a smooth version of reduced rank regression. Their proposal has the form... (the "c+w" stands for "Curds and Whey," the name they gave to their procedure).

name: incremental forward stagewise regression
best_quote: It generates a coefficient profile by repeatedly updating (by a small amount ε) the coefficient of the variable most correlated with the current residuals.

name: infinitesimal forward stagewise regression
best_quote: Letting ε → 0 gives... We call this limiting procedure infinitesimal forward stagewise regression or FS0.

name: l2boost
best_quote: Bühlmann and Hothorn (2008) refer to the same procedure as "L2boost", because of its connections to boosting.

name: dantzig selector
best_quote: Candes and Tao (2007) proposed the following criterion: minimize ||β||_1 subject to ||X^T(y − Xβ)||_∞ ≤ s. They call the solution the Dantzig selector (DS).

name: grouped lasso
best_quote: Since the Euclidean norm of a vector β_ℓ is zero only if all of its components are zero, this procedure encourages sparsity at both the group and individual levels.

name: relaxed lasso
best_quote: Alternatively, one can use the lasso to select the set of non-zero predictors, and then apply the lasso again, but using only the selected predictors from the first step. This is known as the relaxed lasso (Meinshausen, 2007).

name: scad penalty
best_quote: one can modify the lasso penalty function so that larger coefficients are shrunken less severely; the smoothly clipped absolute deviation (SCAD) penalty of Fan and Li (2005) replaces λ|β| by J_a(β, λ)

name: adaptive lasso
best_quote: The adaptive lasso (Zou, 2006) uses a weighted penalty of the form sum w_j|β_j| where w_j = 1/|β̂_j|^ν, β̂_j is the ordinary least squares estimate and ν > 0... yields consistent estimates of the parameters while retaining the attractive convexity property of the lasso.

name: pathwise coordinate descent
best_quote: The idea is to fix the penalty parameter λ in the Lagrangian form (3.52) and optimize successively over each parameter, holding the other parameters fixed at their current values.

name: z-score for regression coefficients
best_quote: To test the hypothesis that a particular coefficient β_j = 0, we form the standardized coefficient or Z-score z_j = β̂_j/sqrt(v_j σ̂^2) where v_j is the jth diagonal element of (X^T X)^{-1}.

name: f-statistic for nested models
best_quote: The F statistic measures the change in residual sum-of-squares per additional parameter in the bigger model, and it is normalized by an estimate of σ^2.

name: variance-covariance matrix of least squares estimates
best_quote: The variance–covariance matrix of the least squares parameter estimates is easily derived from (3.6) and is given by Var(β̂) = (X^T X)^{-1}σ^2.

name: multiple regression coefficient interpretation
best_quote: The multiple regression coefficient β̂_j represents the additional contribution of x_j on y, after x_j has been adjusted for all other predictors.

name: multicollinearity
best_quote: If x_p is highly correlated with some of the other x_k's, the residual vector z_p will be close to zero, and from (3.28) the coefficient β̂_p will be very unstable.

name: bias-variance tradeoff in model selection
best_quote: Prediction accuracy can sometimes be improved by shrinking or setting some coefficients to zero. By doing so we sacrifice a little bit of bias to reduce the variance of the predicted values, and hence may improve the overall prediction accuracy.

name: mean squared error decomposition
best_quote: MSE(θ̃) = E[(θ̃ − θ)^2] = Var(θ̃) + [E(θ̃) − θ]^2. The first term is the variance, while the second term is the squared bias.

name: aic criterion
best_quote: cross-validation to estimate prediction error and select k; the AIC criterion is a popular alternative.

name: l1 arc length
best_quote: For the piecewise-linear LAR coefficient profile, this amounts to summing the L1 norms of the changes in coefficients from step to step.

name: piecewise-linear solution path
best_quote: By construction the coefficients in LAR change in a piecewise linear fashion.
